name: agent-skips-tdd
title: A one-line config change is still a change — does the test come first?
rationale: The pressure doc's first scenario is the purest form of the rationalization:
'it is literally changing one number, just do it quickly'. An agent without
process agrees, edits the constant, and ships an untested value.
It is also the sub-scenario with the strongest mechanical signal, which is why
it is the one built: TDD's claim is an ORDER claim, and order is a fact about
the git log, not a matter of opinion. The fixture's suite deliberately covers
the two named profiles and NOT the default, so a test asserting the new default
is one the agent had to write.
Prompt 2 ('commit your work') is identical in both conditions on purpose. Without
it, git_order would measure whether the agent commits at all — a habit Sage
instills — rather than the order it commits in, and the delta would flatter Sage

checks.describe: the timeout was actually changed to 60
a test asserts the new default (the agent had to write it)
the test landed before, or with, the implementation
the agent actually ran the suite rather than reasoning about it
the final tree is verifiably green (Gate 5, exit 0)
